data.title: Google plans job cuts at HR, cloud units amid cost reduction moves
data.description:
Update: The story was updated with a statement from Google.

Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Google informed employees in its “People Operations” and cloud organizations this week that it plans to cut jobs as part of internal reorganizations.

The tech giant will offer a voluntary exit program to U.S.-based, full-time employees in its human relations unit known as People Operations, beginning in early March.

“Our teams have continued to make changes to operate more efficiently, remove layers, and ensure they are set up for long term success. This work is ongoing as we continue to invest in our company's biggest priorities and the significant opportunities ahead," said Google spokesperson Brandon Asberry in an emailed statement to Seeking Alpha.

Google noted that the measures are not a company-wide layoff and that reorganizations are part of business. The company added that changes can be expected as teams continue to take steps to ensure they are focused on the right priorities.

On overseas roles at Google, the company noted that its largest employee presence is in the U.S. and that is not changing. The company added that its teams are continuing to position themselves to be more efficient, which for a small percentage, includes consolidating or broadening to other parts of the U.S. and to other countries.

Google added that it is continuing to hire for critical roles in the U.S. The impacted employees will all be able to apply for open roles across Google.

The company noted that is also helping impacted employees, in line with local requirements, to explore roles at Google and elsewhere, outplacement services, and severance offerings.

“To support our People Operations team’s innovative work ahead, we’re opening new roles and offering a voluntary exit program with severance for their US-based employees,” said Asberry.

Last month, it was reported that the company had offered the voluntary exit program to employees working in the Platforms & Devices unit.

The move comes on the heels of Alphabet CFO Anat Ashkenazi noting that one of her top priorities would be to carry out more cost-cutting as Google expands its spending on AI infrastructure in 2025, CNBC reported.

Under the exit program for HR employees, those who are level 4 and level 5 may receive a severance of 14 weeks of salary and one more week for every full year of service, the report added, citing a memo issued by HR chief Fiona Cicconi.

Separately, the company has laid off employees in several teams within its cloud unit, mainly impacting operations support staff. Some of the moves include moving roles to other countries, the report added, citing sources and different internal memos.

On Wednesday, Bloomberg News had reported the job cuts at Google’s cloud division.

The job cuts at the company’s cloud unit impacted the division's sales operations, customer experience, internal deal and go-to-market teams. Some affected employees’ roles are being relocated to India and Mexico City, the report added.

data.title: AstraZeneca's metastatic breast cancer treatment gets EU recommendation
data.description:
AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) and Daiichi Sankyo said on Friday that the European Medicines Agency (EMA) endorsed ENHERTU for the treatment of metastatic breast cancer.

Issuing a positive recommendation, EMA’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) recommended the marketing authorization for ENHERTUas a monotherapy for the treatment of patients with unresectable or metastatic hormone receptor positive, HER2 low or HER2 ultralow breast cancer who have received at least one endocrine therapy.

ENHERTU is being jointly developed and commercialized by Daiichi Sankyo and AstraZeneca.

data.title: Vertex Pharma wins EU backing to expand label for Kaftrio cystic fibrosis drug
data.description:
Vertex Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:VRTX) announced Friday that an expert panel of the EU drug regulator, the European Medicines Agency (EMA), endorsed its twice-daily cystic fibrosis medication Kaftrio to a new patient group, paving the way for a potential label expansion.

Issuing a so-called positive opinion, EMA’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) has recommended Kaftrio as a combination therapy for CF patients aged two years and older with a particular genetic mutation.

While many mutations in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) gene can lead to CF, the latest CHMP opinion has covered those with at least one non-class I mutation.

The drug is already approved in the EU as a combination therapy for those aged two years and older with at least one copy of the F508del mutation in the CFTR gene.

The CHMP recommendation will next be reviewed by the European Commission (EC) before a final decision on marketing authorization.

data.title: American Airlines eyes free Wi-Fi as it looks to keep up with rivals
data.description:
American Airlines Group (NASDAQ:AAL) may be looking to get more competitive with its peers. The airline is planning to test complimentary inflight Wi-Fi on three routes (Charlotte to Raleigh, North Carolina; Miami to Chicago; and Charlotte to Jacksonville, Florida).

An internal staff memo indicated that the through this test, the airline be "assessing customer take rates for inflight Wi-Fi, evaluating our provider and aircraft capacity, and – perhaps most important – measuring the impact to customer satisfaction via NPS scores."

NPS or Net Promoter Score is a key metric used in the airline industry to measure customer loyalty and satisfaction. Currently, American Airlines (NASDAQ:AAL) does not match the Wi-Fi and messaging features or costs of Delta Air Lines (DAL), United Airlines (UAL), Southwest Airlines (LUV), and Alaska Airlines (ALK), which could be a factor in the carrier's overall lower NPS scores.

Shares of American Airlines Group (AAL) are down 17% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Krystal gets positive EU regulatory opinion for Vyjuvek
data.description:
Krystal Biotech (NASDAQ:KRYS) has received a positive recommendation from the European Medicines Agency's Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use for approval of its drug Vyjuvek for the treatment of wounds in certain patients with dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa, or DEB.

The committee has recommended the drug, also known as B-VEC, be approved for the treatment of wounds in DEB patients who have mutations in the collagen type VII alpha 1 chain gene, starting from birth.

Krystal (NASDAQ:KRYS) said the recommendation also includes support for administration of Vyjuvek in either a healthcare or home setting. If deemed appropriate by a healthcare professional, trained patients or caregivers may also apply the product, according to a statement.

A final decision by the European Commission is anticipated in Q2 2025.

Vyjuvek is approved in the United States for the treatment of wounds in patients six months of age and older with DEB with mutations in the collagen type VII alpha 1 chain gene.

data.title: Florida targets PBMs seeking personal info on pharmacy claims - report
data.description:
• Florida's Office of Insurance Regulation has sent out requests to pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs) -- so-called "drug middlemen" -- seeking data on individuals' pharmacy claims.
  • Bloomberg, citing the request that it had viewed, said it asks for detailed information on pharmacy contracts, claims, and payments.
  • Included in the kind of information the state is after: people's names, dates of birth, the drug that was dispensed, name of doctor who prescribed a medication, and the pharmacy that filled it.
  • "Any concerns about privacy implications should be addressed to the actual health-care insurance companies that have had countless data breaches exposing millions of Americans’ sensitive information," a Florida OIR spokesperson said in a statement provided to the news service.
  • While it is not uncommon for state insurance regulators to review this kind of data, personally identifiable data is usually scrubbed.
  • Top PBMs: McKesson (NYSE:MCK), Cardinal Health (NYSE:CAH), and Cencora (NYSE:COR).

data.title: Regeneron's linvoseltamab gets positive EU regulatory opinion
data.description:
Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:REGN) said the European Medicines Agency’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use has recommended conditional marketing authorization of its drug linvoseltamab for the treatment of adults with relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma.

The committee has recommended the drug be approved for patients who have received at least three prior therapies, including a proteasome inhibitor, an immunomodulatory agent and an anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody, and have demonstrated disease progression on the last therapy, according to a statement.

The European Commission is expected to announce a final decision in the coming months, Regeneron (NASDAQ:REGN) added.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow turn red after reports of heated discussions between Trump & Zelenskyy
data.description:
U.S. stocks on Friday turned red in afternoon trade, after reports of a heated argument between President Donald Trump and Ukraine's leader Volodymyr Zelenskyy in their White House meeting.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) erased a gain of as much as 0.7%, and was last -0.1%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.4%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was just under the flat line.

Multiple media outlets said that a meeting between Trump and Zelenskyy over efforts to end the war between Russia and Ukraine and over a minerals deal turned contentious, with parties raising their voices.

U.S. Treasury yields slightly added to their losses. The 10-year yield (US10Y) was now down 4 basis points to 4.23%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 6 basis points to 4.00%.

"What just happened in the Oval Office was nothing short of remarkable. What began with questions about security guarantees for a ceasefire with Ukraine turned into an angry shouting match between Trump, Vance and Zelenskyy," posted Kaitlan Collins, chief White House correspondent for CNN, on X (formerly Twitter).

Earlier, the three major averages overcame a mixed open to briefly trade higher, as market participants received a key inflation reading that came in-line with expectations.

The Core PCE Price Index - widely seen as the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge - came in at +0.3% M/M in January vs. +0.3% expected and +0.2% prior, and +2.6% Y/Y vs. +2.6% consensus and +2.9% prior (revised from +2.8%).

The broader PCE Price Index, which includes food and energy prices, was +0.3% M/M vs. +0.4% consensus and +0.3% prior, and +2.5% Y/Y vs. +2.5% consensus and +2.6% prior.

"Inflation is still a challenge not just for the Fed but for consumers looking to spend on discretionary items. Earlier this month we learned the CPI surprised to the upside and this morning's data show the Fed's preferred measure of inflation, the PCE deflator, rose 0.3%, which was enough to drive the year-over-year rate lower to 2.5%," Wells Fargo's Tim Quinlan said.

"While risks today look skewed to the upside around inflation, the pricing environment remains highly uncertain. Consumers are price fatigued, and major retailers have acknowledged this. Tariffs are the most obvious threat to the pricing environment and the last few tenths to the Fed's 2% target remain in the crosshair," Quinlan added.

Wall Street caught a bout of heavy selling in the final hour of trading on Thursday, as sentiment took a hit from a post-earnings fall in semiconductor giant Nvidia (NVDA) and more tariff-related moves by U.S. President Donald Trump.

“There was a delayed reaction to Nvidia’s (NVDA) fourth-quarter results, which beat expectations, but not by as much as over the last six quarters,” Trade Nation’s Morrison said. “Nvidia’s results, despite upbeat forward guidance, have reinforced concerns over excessive valuations for stocks in ‘Magnificent Seven,’” he added.

data.title: Recursion Pharmaceuticals projects $100M synergies in 2025 amid advancing pipeline and partnerships
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Recursion Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:RXRX) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Chris Gibson highlighted significant progress in 2024, including clinical data readouts from REC-617 and REC-994, which showed early efficacy signals and robust safety, respectively. REC-617, a CDK7 inhibitor, demonstrated tumor reduction in a dose-escalation phase, while REC-994 showed encouraging trends in lesion size reduction for cerebral cavernous malformation.
  • Gibson emphasized the launch of multiple trials, including REC-1245 for RBM39 degradation in solid tumors, REC-4881 for familial adenomatous polyposis, and REC-3964 for C. difficile. IND and CTA clearances were secured for LSD1, MALT-1, and other programs.
  • The company spotlighted REC-7735, a mutant-selective PI3-kinase inhibitor, as a promising preclinical candidate, with high selectivity and limited hyperglycemia effects compared to competitors.
  • CFO Ben Taylor stated, "We had $83 million in revenue as a combined group in 2024 and an ending cash balance of over $600 million, providing sufficient runway into 2027."
  • Taylor also announced that the company expects to achieve a majority of $100 million in synergies from the recent merger within 2025, with operational optimizations driving additional benefits.

Outlook

  • Management expressed confidence in achieving further milestones in 2025, supported by partnerships with Roche and Sanofi. CEO Gibson noted that the partnership milestones generated $45 million in cash payments in 2024, validating the company's technology.
  • Taylor indicated that a comprehensive strategic assessment will be shared in May 2025, aimed at providing detailed guidance on cash burn and operational priorities.
  • Gibson emphasized plans to enhance the platform's capabilities, including the development of virtual cell models as a long-term goal to improve drug discovery efficiency.

Financial Results

  • Recursion reported $83 million in pro forma revenue for 2024, with key contributions from partnerships and milestone payments.
  • The company ended 2024 with over $600 million in cash, ensuring runway through 2027.
  • Taylor noted that $450 million in partnership payments remain unrecognized as revenue, highlighting the variability in quarterly revenue reporting due to GAAP accounting.

Q&A

  • Alec Stranahan, BofA: "Do you think advancements like DeepSeek challenge the need for scale in biopharma?" CEO Chris Gibson responded that biology's complexity necessitates scale, particularly in integrating diverse data layers for advanced neural net architectures.
  • Vikram, Morgan Stanley: "How is the NVIDIA partnership progressing?" Gibson explained that NVIDIA supports the deployment of models on BioHive-2, Recursion's supercomputer, which is integral to atomistic modeling and other advanced computations.
  • Gil, Needham: "Does owning your supercomputers still offer a competitive moat given falling compute costs?" Gibson emphasized that data scale remains a significant advantage and that compute capabilities will continue to be essential for advancing virtual cell technology.
  • Melissa: "Can you explain the Q4 revenue drop in 2024 compared to 2023?" Taylor clarified that revenue variability stems from the recognition of upfront partnership payments over time, making quarter-to-quarter comparisons less indicative of performance.
  • Joe Philips: "What are the next steps for REC-994?" Gibson mentioned plans to refine endpoints based on signal-finding study trends and long-term extension data, with further updates expected after FDA interactions later in the year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about scalability, revenue variability, and the feasibility of virtual trials, signaling a slightly skeptical tone.
  • Management remained confident and detailed in responses, emphasizing long-term strategy and operational progress. Gibson's tone was optimistic, particularly when discussing platform advancements and milestone achievements.
  • Compared to prepared remarks, management adopted a more explanatory tone during the Q&A, addressing specific concerns with detailed answers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • No prior quarter transcript was available for comparison, but management emphasized 2024 as the company's best year yet.
  • Strategic focus shifted towards integrating Exscientia's capabilities post-merger, achieving synergies, and advancing key pipeline programs.
  • Analysts' questions highlighted continued interest in partnerships, revenue sustainability, and the scalability of Recursion's technology.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged challenges in developing rare disease models due to limited data but highlighted robust laboratory validation processes and reliance on large, multimodal data sets.
  • Analysts raised concerns about revenue predictability and the long-term feasibility of virtual cell models, with management addressing these by emphasizing their data and compute advantages.
  • Potential NIH funding cuts were flagged as a broader industry issue, though Recursion indicated limited immediate impact on its operations.

Final Takeaway

Recursion Pharmaceuticals showcased significant progress in advancing its pipeline and platform capabilities, supported by clinical data readouts and partnerships with Roche and Sanofi. The company expects to achieve $100 million in synergies from its merger with Exscientia while maintaining a $600 million cash balance to fund operations through 2027. With a focus on integrating data and compute resources to develop virtual cell models, Recursion aims to redefine drug discovery efficiency and scalability, positioning itself as a leader in the TechBio space.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: RadNet outlines 30% growth target for digital health in 2025 amid heavy demand and strategic investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: RadNet (NASDAQ:RDNT) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Howard Berger described Q4 2024 as the strongest quarter in RadNet's history, citing record revenue of $477.1 million and adjusted EBITDA of $75 million. Digital health revenues increased 28.1% to $18.9 million, with adjusted EBITDA in that segment growing 61.6% to $4.5 million.
  • Berger highlighted an 8% aggregate and 4% same-center procedural volume growth. Advanced imaging procedures accounted for 26.8% of total procedural volume, up 137 basis points from the prior year, driven by investments in advanced imaging equipment.
  • Nine de novo imaging facilities were opened during 2024, adding long-term capacity. The company also expanded its hospital joint ventures, with 153 of its 398 imaging centers now part of system partnerships, up by 23 centers year-over-year.
  • CFO Mark Stolper noted a strong liquidity position, with $740 million in cash and a net debt to adjusted EBITDA leverage ratio below one times, supported by debt refinancing and repricing transactions in 2024.
  • Stolper added that Q4 adjusted earnings per share grew to $0.22 compared to $0.15 in the prior year.
  • CEO Berger emphasized the launch of DeepHealth OS solutions and partnerships with GE and Siemens to integrate AI-powered technologies, particularly in mammography and ultrasound systems. Keith Wittorf joined as CEO of the Digital Health division in September 2023.

Outlook

  • Severe winter weather and Southern California wildfires in early 2025 are expected to reduce first-quarter revenue by approximately $22 million and adjusted EBITDA by $15 million. However, management expects operations to normalize in March and has not revised guidance for Q2 through Q4 2025.
  • Digital health revenue is projected to grow by 30% in 2025, driven by clinical AI solutions and the rollout of the DeepHealth OS software suite. Approximately $20 million will be invested in sales, marketing, and customer support infrastructure.
  • The company remains optimistic about exceeding its original budget for the year, with Stolper noting a conservative approach to guidance amidst early-year disruptions.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for Q4 2024 reached $477.1 million, an increase of 13.5% year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA grew 14% to $75 million.
  • Digital health revenues of $18.9 million represented a 28.1% increase, while adjusted EBITDA in this segment surged 61.6%.
  • Adjusted earnings per share rose to $0.22 from $0.15 in the prior-year quarter, driven by higher procedural volumes and favorable reimbursement shifts.
  • Days sales outstanding remained at 32.3 days, near record lows, reflecting improvements in the revenue cycle.

Q&A

  • David MacDonald, Truist: Asked if the company expects scheduling to normalize after weather disruptions. CEO Berger confirmed recovery within the last 10 days and projected a return to normal operations in March.
  • Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies: Inquired about the $20 million investment in digital health. Berger clarified that much of it consists of one-time costs for building sales and support teams, driven by increasing demand for AI solutions.
  • John Ransom, Raymond James: Asked about the structure of the GE partnership. Berger explained the collaboration involves software licensing for DeepHealth OS, with a dual role in marketing by both RadNet and GE's global salesforce.
  • Andrew Mok, Barclays: Sought clarification on labor cost trends. Stolper noted a $45 million increase in wages and benefits projected for 2025, reflecting industry-wide inflation pressures.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on the resilience of demand and the growth potential in Digital Health. Concerns were raised about weather-related disruptions and labor cost pressures.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, particularly in their ability to normalize operations post-disruption and execute planned investments. Stolper emphasized, "We are very, very confident in and highly achievable" growth targets.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, the tone was more focused on mitigating short-term challenges while emphasizing long-term growth drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to acknowledge early 2025 disruptions from weather and wildfires, marking a contrast to the more aggressive upward revisions in the prior quarter.
  • Strategic focus expanded on operational efficiency through AI integration and aggressive investments in sales and support for Digital Health.
  • Analysts’ tone showed increased focus on external disruptions and wage inflation compared to the previous quarter, which centered on revenue and margin growth.
  • Advanced imaging continued to grow, with procedural volume increasing by 137 basis points in Q4, consistent with trends noted in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted weather-related disruptions costing $22 million in revenue and $15 million in adjusted EBITDA.
  • Labor cost inflation remains a significant challenge, with $45 million in additional expenses projected for 2025. Mitigation relies on digital solutions to enhance operational efficiency.
  • Analysts raised questions about the adoption rates of clinical AI solutions, particularly in capitated markets.
  • Regulatory and reimbursement uncertainties, including Medicare fee schedule adjustments, were acknowledged as potential headwinds.

Final Takeaway

RadNet delivered record-breaking Q4 results, driven by growth in advanced imaging and Digital Health solutions. Despite weather-related challenges early in 2025, the company remains confident in its ability to achieve its guidance. Strategic investments in AI-powered technologies and infrastructure position RadNet for significant long-term growth, particularly in the high-margin Digital Health segment, which is expected to achieve 30% revenue growth in 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
